budget_ledger.seq_cut_read_as: `seq K : Q B.` is a budget ledger entry: Q is the cut assertion and B is the budget assigned to the budget-carrying branch. For scaled-probability goals, the safe shape must also carry the source probability slot P. Missing B, missing P, or `1%r` can be accepted while charging the wrong branch.

frontier_alignment.summary: Frontier alignment: sampling steps are visible inside the frontier. Use this as a proof-reader map before choosing while/rnd/seq/call tactics.

first_instruction_alignment.right_first: no matching right-side sample at this frontier
first_instruction_alignment.proof_read: The visible frontier is one-sided; use a one-sided tactic, alignment step, or seq cut before applying a symmetric tactic.

rows.right: no matching right-side sample at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is a sampling frontier; choose a coupling or distribution argument that connects the sampled values.

procedure_navigation.how_to_use: Positions for choosing wp/sp depth, which `if` to rcond, and where the next loop/call frontier is. Facts only — pick the tactic and the branch direction yourself.

checks: Read coverage and missing live facts before committing a seq cut.
A runnable seq candidate is not evidence that the invariant is strong enough.

evidence: distribution or lossless token remains visible
obligation_families.effect: The remaining pure goal may include invertibility or lossless side conditions from an earlier sampling alignment.
obligation_families.limitations: No distribution lemma is selected by this surface.

obligation_families.effect: The pure goal exposes tuple/record projections or constructor equalities that can connect program variables to map/list keys.
obligation_families.limitations: Projection equalities are reported only when visible in the current text.

pure_tail_surface.effect: Summarizes verifier-visible pure-goal structure after program frontier actions have been discharged or hidden by local proof steps.
limitations: This panel records syntactic and proof-state facts from the current view.
It does not choose a tactic or instantiate a lemma.

selected_handles.title: Probability budget ledger
selected_handles.why_relevant: A product probability budget is visible; check the budget/event shape before treating a locally accepted sampling, split, or call step as proof progress.

event_obligations.event: (g ^ G1.u, G1.g_ ^ G1.u', g ^ r', g ^ r) \in G3.cilog [146|check]>

candidate_lemma_handles.why: `size L <= n` plus a per-point mass bound can turn `mu dt (mem L)` into `n%r * r`.

route_shape: Keep the product budget at the event level before committing local sampling cleanup.
Use the visible list-size bound to reduce membership in the list to a size times point-mass bound.
Prove the per-point mass bound from the sampled distributions and support exclusions.
Only then descend into local sampling obligations if the per-point subgoal is explicit.
event_bound_bridge.per_point_budget_hint: After applying the list-size bridge with `PKE_.qD`, prove a per-point mass bound whose product with `PKE_.qD%r` is no larger than `(PKE_.qD%r / order%r) ^ 3 * (PKE_.qD%r / (order - 1)%r)`.
event_bound_bridge.anti_route: Do not treat an accepted multi-`rnd` probe as proof-route quality unless it exposes the per-point membership bound required by the list-size lemma.
event_bound_bridge.read_as: A bridge for membership events: the list-size fact caps how many targets can be hit, and the per-point mass bound pays for one target.

route_plan: first apply list-size times point-mass bridge; do not start with local call/seq cleanup
use `size G3.cilog <= PKE_.qD` as the list cardinality side fact
then prove a per-point mass bound for one fixed tuple/list element
spend denominator factors by sampled variable and distribution family
only descend to `rnd` after the per-point mass obligation is explicit

sample_factor_assignments.read_as: `dt` is a finite uniform sample; it should pay an `1/order` point-mass obligation, matched to `PKE_.qD%r / order%r`.
`dt \ pred1 G1.u` is a uniform-except-one sample; it should pay an `1/(order-1)` point-mass obligation, matched to `PKE_.qD%r / (order - 1)%r`.
`dt` is a finite uniform sample; it should pay an `1/order` point-mass obligation, matched to `PKE_.qD%r / order%r`.
`dt` is a finite uniform sample; it should pay an `1/order` point-mass obligation, matched to `PKE_.qD%r / order%r`.

point_mass_route.factor_assignment_summary: point-mass assignment: G1.u -> B1, G1.u' -> B4, r' -> B2, r -> B3
point_mass_route.per_point_mass_read: After the list-size bridge with `PKE_.qD`, the remaining route is a per-point bound for the sampled tuple, paid by the listed sample/factor assignments.

anti_routes.reason: `seq K : membership 1%r` may be accepted while moving the small product budget away from the list-membership bridge.
A call invariant that merely preserves `tuple in L` does not explain the size-times-point-mass budget.

candidate_lemma_handles.why: `size L <= n` plus a per-point mass bound can turn `mu dt (mem L)` into `n%r * r`.

assertion_must_contain.event: (g ^ G1.u, G1.g_ ^ G1.u', g ^ r', g ^ r) \in G3.cilog [146|check]>
assertion_must_contain.next_after_boundary: return to the list-size times point-mass ledger; the boundary does not discharge the product budget

allowed_boundary_moves.safe_when: Use only when the call proof obligation preserves the same membership event across the abstract call frontier.

route_plan: stay_at_probability_or_event_level
decompose product budget into factor obligations
use list-size / point-mass bridge before local rnd cleanup
apply list-membership size-times-point-mass bridge first
assign sampled variables to point-mass factor slots before call/seq cleanup
descend to sampling only after per-point bound is explicit

unsafe_lowerings.reason: A single accepted `rnd` step, or a compact `do N!rnd`, may leave a subgoal that charges local samples against the entire product budget.
EasyCrypt may accept `phoare split !`, but the residual goals can still be a bad route under a product budget.
A plausible `seq K : side_fact` can move the small product budget onto a high-probability prefix or deterministic side condition.
Direct `call (_: true).` under a one-sided `[<=]` bound can fail because EasyCrypt expects a compatible bound direction.

budget_ledger.read_as: A probability budget ledger: factor slots are the resources in the product bound, and event obligations are the proof events that should spend those resources before local sampling cleanup.

likely_proof_family: list-membership event bound via size times point-mass
split the event/budget before descending into procedure proof

useful_fact_handles.why: The group order denominator appears in the probability budget.
The query-bound numerator appears in the probability budget.
Product-bound side conditions often need non-negativity of factors.
Ratio factors in the bound usually need numerator/denominator non-negativity.
Powered ratio factors often need exponent non-negativity closure.
Strict positivity is useful when rewriting ratio-over-itself side conditions.
Strictly positive denominator/numerator facts support positive ratio factors.
Powered positive ratio factors stay positive.
B/B residuals often close by rewriting `divrr` after proving B is nonzero.
Membership-event probability bounds often use list-size or union-bound style lemmas when available.

side_condition_recipe.name: B_over_B residual
side_condition_recipe.read_as: When a residual becomes a budget divided by the same budget, treat it as B/B: establish B <> 0 or B > 0, then rewrite with `divrr`.

facts_and_diagnostics.fact_note: Only current-frontier facts are shown here; the full proof context artifact keeps the compiler/internal details.

probability_budget.read_as: Advisory middle-end context for route selection; this is not a proof script or tactic order.

notes.message: One-sided sampling residual: G1.u{1} <$ dt. The postcondition still mentions the sampled value or a quantified witness, so this may be a true losslessness side condition or leftover coupling debt.

evidence: recent accepted/probed `seq` cut may allocate the product budget to the wrong branch
route memory contains previously bad probability-budget tactic shapes

related_checkpoints.label: Before product-budget seq cut #3
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: Revisit this product-budget seq cut; the current probability budget residual may need a stronger midpoint assertion or a different budget allocation.

related_checkpoints.label: Before product-budget seq cut #3
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: Revisit this product-budget seq cut; the current probability budget residual may need a stronger midpoint assertion or a different budget allocation.

related_checkpoints.label: Before seq cut #7
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: seq-cut boundary; selecting it restores the proof state before this cut was committed

limitations: does not infer a replacement invariant, cut formula, or postcondition
uses only current residual evidence and recent local repair failures

rewrite_anchor.current_tactic: seq 13 : (size G3.cilog <= PKE_.qD /\ G1.w <> zero /\ G1.g_ = g ^ G1.w) 1%r ((PKE_.qD%r / order%r) ^ 3 * (PKE_.qD%r / (order - 1)%r)) 0%r 1%r.

proof_piece_memory.interpretation: This is manager-owned evidence about prior route repairs and daemon-verified route options. Replayable pieces should still be verifier-checked before commit; stale and negative pieces are warnings, not tactics.

rewrite_anchor.current_tactic: seq 13 : (size G3.cilog <= PKE_.qD /\ G1.w <> zero /\ G1.g_ = g ^ G1.w) 1%r ((PKE_.qD%r / order%r) ^ 3 * (PKE_.qD%r / (order - 1)%r)) 0%r 1%r.

lineage_briefing.interpretation: Manager-owned continuity notes for this node. Use them as resume/navigation evidence; EasyCrypt replay still decides whether any old route piece is valid.

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need parsed goal shape, resolved names, or KB pattern hints.
The current cut or frontier context may expose a call route.
ask_manager_for.returns: call-ready/oracle-equiv context for the current frontier, plus the BODY of the procedure(s) called here (functor aliases resolved) so you can see which oracles/state your call invariant must preserve

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are considering `call (_: Inv)` and already have a concrete invariant expression you want to test first.
ask_manager_for.returns: a read-only preview of the obligations caused by that candidate invariant, including pre/init, call-preservation, oracle-side, postcondition, missing-fact signals, and extra conjuncts that may create avoidable obligations

ask_manager_for.use_when: The visible cut may depend on LHS/RHS statement alignment or missing live facts.
ask_manager_for.returns: swap/alignment context for the visible pRHL frontier

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the valid form for call, seq, while, rnd, or rewrite.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: The visible cut/frontier may need indexed `sp i j` before branch tactics.
Mid-proof pRHL suffix surgery may need indexed `wp`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix-alignment traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp`, `wp`, or `sim`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relation before `sim`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `conseq` forms and pRHL memory-annotation traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: One side may have an extra sample or need an explicit sample coupling.
A known statement-order mismatch may need an eager/lazy transformation.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `eager` forms and when to prefer smaller surgery

items.label: Restore before last checkpoint rewind
items.why_checkpoint: state saved before the last checkpoint rewind; selecting it returns to that proof state
items.effect_if_selected: This restores the branch to the proof state saved before the last checkpoint rewind.

items.label: Before product-budget seq cut #3
items.committed_tactic: seq 21 : ((G3.a, G3.a_, G3.c, G3.d) \in G3.cilog) ((PKE_.qD%r / order%r) ^ 3 * (PKE_.qD%r / (order - 1)%r)) 1%r 1%r 0%r.
items.why_checkpoint: Revisit this product-budget seq cut; the current probability budget residual may need a stronger midpoint assertion or a different budget allocation.
items.effect_if_selected: Undo committed tactic #3 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

items.label: Before latest branch-local tactic #10
items.why_checkpoint: latest branch-local step inside the current seq scope
items.effect_if_selected: Undo committed tactic #10 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

items.label: After seq opened / before branch work #8
items.why_checkpoint: seq-local branch boundary; selecting it keeps the seq cut and removes branch-local work after it
items.effect_if_selected: Undo committed tactic #8 and every committed tactic after it in this node.

items.label: Before seq cut #7
items.committed_tactic: seq 13 : (size G3.cilog <= PKE_.qD /\ G1.w <> zero /\ G1.g_ = g ^ G1.w) 1%r ((PKE_.qD%r / order%r) ^ 3 * (PKE_.qD%r / (order - 1)%r)) 0%r 1%r.
items.why_checkpoint: seq-cut boundary; selecting it restores the proof state before this cut was committed
items.effect_if_selected: Undo committed tactic #7 and every committed tactic after it in this node.
